critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression hospital readmission experiment has four critical methodological problems.

1. Random stratified split on multi-year temporal EHR data is the wrong split strategy. The design uses 'stratified random sampling across all available months' with the stated rationale that this 'balances representation from different time periods in each set.' But balancing temporal representation in train and test is opposite to the deployment scenario: in deployment, the model is trained on historical data and predicts future admissions. If patients admitted in months 25-36 appear in the training set and patients admitted in months 1-12 appear in the test set, the model is evaluated on its ability to predict past outcomes from future-trained features — which is not what deployment requires. Evidence: implement a proper temporal holdout (first 70% of time period = train, next 15% = validation, final 15% = test) and compare AUC-ROC; the gap measures optimism in the random split.

2. Vital sign aggregation removes the temporal signal that supposedly justifies the RNN. The design states 'Vital sign features: Mean, standard deviation, minimum, maximum, and linear trend slope for each vital sign over the full hospitalization.' These are summary statistics that collapse the temporal sequence into scalars. The logistic regression receives these same aggregated features. The RNN supposedly processes raw temporal sequences, but the feature engineering section describes only aggregated statistics, not raw time-series sequences. If the RNN also receives aggregated statistics (as the baseline does), there is no architectural difference — both models receive the same feature vector. If the RNN receives raw sequences, the feature engineering section is incomplete. Evidence: clarify explicitly whether the RNN receives raw vital sign time-series (4-hour interval sequences) or aggregated statistics; if aggregated, the comparison is not between sequential and non-sequential modeling.

3. Preprocessing fit on training set only but imputed with per-patient mean — which patients constitute training. The design states 'missing vital sign measurements (handled by per-patient mean imputation fit on training data only).' Per-patient mean imputation for a single patient uses that patient's own observed vital signs to fill gaps in their own record. This is not leakage from test patients, but it does mean that patients with many missing values receive imputed means that are unreliable for patients with few observations. More critically, the 'fit on training data only' language implies the imputation strategy was determined on training data — but per-patient mean imputation does not require population-level fitting, only the patient's own data. The leakage risk here is if population-level imputation strategies (e.g., population means for patients with zero observations) use test data. Evidence: specify the fallback for patients with entirely missing vital sign measurements; if population-level means are used as fallback and those means include test patients, there is leakage.

4. AUC-ROC as primary metric is appropriate but the design does not report results with confidence intervals or statistical significance tests. With a 15% test set from 100,000 records (approximately 15,000 test admissions), the AUC-ROC confidence interval is approximately ±0.003-0.005. Small AUC differences (e.g., RNN=0.82 vs. LR=0.81) may be statistically significant at this sample size but clinically meaningless. The design states metrics 'are reported with 95% confidence intervals' but does not pre-specify the minimum clinically meaningful AUC difference that would constitute a confirmatory finding. Without a minimum effect size, any statistically significant positive result — no matter how small — would be claimed as supporting the hypothesis. Evidence: pre-specify a minimum AUC difference (e.g., 0.02 AUC points) as the threshold for practical significance; statistical significance alone at n=15,000 does not constitute meaningful evidence.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified split on temporal EHR data is wrong for deployment scenario — test patients may predate some training patients in time
Vital sign feature engineering produces only aggregated statistics, not raw sequences — RNN may not actually receive temporal sequences, making comparison non-architectural
Per-patient mean imputation falls back to population means for missing patients — if population means include test patients, there is leakage
No pre-specified minimum effect size for AUC-ROC — statistical significance at n=15,000 does not guarantee practical significance
Stratified random split on 36-month time-series data mixes temporal order; later admissions can appear in training while earlier ones appear in test
RNN receives raw sequences while logistic regression receives aggregates; performance difference conflates information richness with architectural advantage
Vital sign aggregation destroys clinically relevant temporal structure; the logistic regression baseline is built on uninformative summaries
Planned readmission exclusion relies on retrospective designation that is inconsistently coded in EHR, introducing systematic label noise
Single-hospital evaluation cannot separate generalizable clinical signal from site-specific EHR coding and workflow memorization
Stratified random split does not simulate deployment scenario (future prediction); temporal split is needed for valid evaluation
Feature comparison is imprecise: RNN has access to full time-series ordering while LR has moment-based summaries; claim of equivalent information access is incorrect
Per-patient mean imputation from training set ignores patient-specific vital sign baselines; particularly problematic for RNN hidden-state propagation
Readmission outcome definition does not specify whether inter-facility transfers in multi-hospital system are captured or excluded
